name: Alcmene
description: Alcmene was a mortal princess renowned for her beauty, virtue, and unwavering loyalty. She was the daughter of Electryon, king of Mycenae, and Anaxo. Married to Amphitryon, a Theban general, she insisted he avenge the deaths of her brothers before consummating their marriage. While Amphitryon was away, Zeus, captivated by her beauty and virtue, disguised himself as Amphitryon and visited her. That night, she conceived Heracles by Zeus and Iphicles by the returning Amphitryon. Alcmene's unique situation as the mother of a demigod destined for greatness cemented her place in Greek mythology. She embodies the qualities of a virtuous and loyal wife, chosen by Zeus for her exceptional character, and her lineage is inextricably linked to the greatest hero of Greek legend. After her death, she was transformed, according to some accounts, into a stone or a star, demonstrating her enduring significance. The story of Alcmene highlights themes of divine intervention, mortal virtue, and the extraordinary consequences that can arise from the interaction between gods and humans. Her legacy lives on through her son, Heracles, and her tale serves as a testament to the power of fate and the enduring impact of moral integrity, even in the face of divine encounters.
subtitle: Mother of Heracles, Chosen by Zeus

keyMyths.title: The Conception of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Zeus, disguised as Amphitryon, visited Alcmene and fathered Heracles. This divine conception marked the beginning of Heracles' extraordinary destiny and Alcmene's pivotal role in Greek mythology.

keyMyths.title: Alcmene's Test of Amphitryon
keyMyths.description: Alcmene demanded that Amphitryon avenge her brothers' deaths before their marriage could be consummated. This act demonstrates her strength and determination, as well as her loyalty to her family.

keyMyths.title: Transformation After Death
keyMyths.description: Some accounts say that after her death, Alcmene was transformed into a stone or a star. This transformation signifies her enduring legacy and her elevated status due to her role in the Heracles myth.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive source for Greek mythology, detailing Alcmene's lineage and the circumstances of Heracles' birth.
sources.text: A comprehensive source for Greek mythology, detailing Alcmene's lineage and the circumstances of Heracles' birth.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides an early account of Heracles' parentage and Alcmene's role in his conception.
sources.text: Provides an early account of Heracles' parentage and Alcmene's role in his conception.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Offers a version of Alcmene's transformation after death.
sources.text: Offers a version of Alcmene's transformation after death.

associations.name: Loyalty
associations.significance: Alcmene's unwavering loyalty to her husband, Amphitryon, is a defining characteristic, tested by Zeus's deception.

associations.name: Zeus
associations.significance: Her union with Zeus resulted in the birth of Heracles, linking her to divine power and destiny.

associations.name: Mother of Heroes
associations.significance: As the mother of Heracles, she embodies the role of a mother who births extraordinary heroes shaping the world.

associations.name: Beauty
associations.significance: Alcmene's renowned beauty attracted the attention of Zeus, altering her fate.

associations.name: Resilience
associations.significance: She displayed resilience in the face of extraordinary circumstances, including deception and divine encounters.

associations.name: Vengeance
associations.significance: Her demand for vengeance for her brothers' deaths highlights her commitment to justice and honor.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Alcmene and Frigg experience deception related to their husbands and have powerful offspring with divine connections.
Like Alcmene, Devaki is a virtuous woman who gives birth to a divine hero (Krishna) under extraordinary circumstances.
Isis, like Alcmene, is a devoted wife and mother who uses her cunning and strength to protect her son, Horus, from threats.

worshipPractices.name: Hero Shrines
worshipPractices.description: While not a goddess, Alcmene was honored with hero shrines, particularly in Thebes, where people sought her blessings for fertility and protection, acknowledging her role as the mother of Heracles.

cultural.modernLegacy: Alcmene's story continues to inspire modern literature, art, and film, representing themes of motherhood, divine intervention, and the birth of heroes. She symbolizes the strength and virtue of mortal women in the face of extraordinary events.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Royal Lineage
extendedContent.content: Alcmene was born into a royal family as the daughter of King Electryon of Mycenae and Anaxo. Her lineage connected her to Perseus, a significant hero in Greek mythology. Renowned for her exceptional beauty and virtue, Alcmene attracted many suitors. She was chosen by Amphitryon, a Theban general, as his wife. However, Alcmene set a condition for their marriage: Amphitryon had to avenge the deaths of her brothers who were slain during a cattle raid by the Teleboans. This demand showcased her strong sense of justice and loyalty to her family. While Amphitryon embarked on his mission, Alcmene's fate took an extraordinary turn when Zeus, the king of the gods, became captivated by her.

extendedContent.title: The Deception of Zeus and the Birth of Heracles
extendedContent.content: While Amphitryon was away avenging Alcmene's brothers, Zeus, disguised as Amphitryon, visited Alcmene. He prolonged the night, making it last three times as long, and consummated his union with her. When the real Amphitryon returned, Alcmene was confused by his presence, leading to the revelation of Zeus's deception. As a result of this divine encounter, Alcmene conceived Heracles, who was destined to become the greatest of the Greek heroes. She also conceived Iphicles with Amphitryon. Hera, Zeus's jealous wife, attempted to delay Heracles's birth, but Alcmene was aided by Galanthis, her servant, who tricked Hera. This event marked the beginning of Heracles's legendary life, forever linking Alcmene to the hero's destiny and struggles. The birth of Heracles solidified Alcmene's place in mythology as the mother of a demigod.

extendedContent.title: Later Life and Legacy
extendedContent.content: After the death of Amphitryon, Alcmene's story takes various turns depending on the source. Some accounts say she married Rhadamanthus, son of Zeus and brother of Minos, after Amphitryon's death. Upon her death, accounts vary. Some say she died in Thebes, while others say she was taken to the Isles of the Blessed. One account describes how the Heracleidae (descendants of Heracles) fled to Athens seeking protection from Eurystheus. Alcmene plays a role in these events, further highlighting her importance in the lineage of Heracles. Alcmene's legacy is primarily as the mother of Heracles. Her story embodies themes of divine intervention, motherhood, and the birth of heroes. She remains a symbol of strength, virtue, and the extraordinary circumstances that can shape the lives of mortals.

shortDescription: Alcmene, a mortal princess, was the mother of Heracles by Zeus. Known for her virtue and beauty, her story highlights divine intervention and the birth of heroes.
